title: The Robots Cometh
excerpt: The humanoid revolution is coming—and the Chinese firm Unitree is leading the charge.
content_html:
Spectators typically leave a UFC fight with ringing ears and a sheepish resolve to get back to the gym. Wang Xingxing went straight to his laboratory. 

“I thought I could make a bigger robot for boxing competitions,” says Wang, the founder and CEO of Hangzhou-based humanoid-robotics firm Unitree. “Then I thought, Why not do an even bigger one that can actually carry a human inside?”

The upshot of Wang’s creative fervor is the GD01, which Unitree bills as the world’s first mass-produced, transformable mecha robot. Standing 9 ft. tall and weighing half a ton, it can transport a pilot in its torso-mounted cockpit, walk on either two legs or four, and wield fists powerful enough to knock down walls. Heavy-duty mechanical hinges connect titanium-alloy limbs to a protective casing of carbon fiber, enabling smooth, deliberate strides on brutish red legs.

Wang showed off the GD01 exclusively to TIME as part of his first international media interview, revealing that beyond mixed martial arts, he took a dash of inspiration from James Cameron: “We actually drew some references from a similar robot featured in Avatar.”

Wang has done more than arguably anyone else to bring this science fiction closer to reality. A native of China’s coastal city of Ningbo, he founded Unitree in 2016 when he was just 26 years old and has moved from robot dogs to humanoid robots and now, with GD01, human mobility machines. But while the GD01 might inspire awe, humbler humanoids like Unitree’s flagship G1 are the ones set to become the workhorses of a global robotics transformation already gathering pace in China, the U.S., and beyond.

This is the next great tech frontier, one that has previously been the purview of Star Wars and Blade Runner: AI that walks, sees, hears and physically interacts with the real world. Morgan Stanley predicts that as many as 13 million humanoids could walk among us by 2035—equivalent to the population of Bolivia. That number is forecast to rise to 1 billion by 2050, when the industry could be worth $5 trillion. 

Unitree is hardly alone staking a claim in this space. In January, Tesla announced the third generation of its Optimus humanoid robot with a goal to eventually produce 10 million units per year at factories in Fremont and Texas. In late June, California-based Figure dispatched its latest 03 humanoid to BMW’s Spartanburg plant in South Carolina, where its earlier 02 model helped assemble 30,000 cars last year. Oregon-based Agility, meanwhile, has secured deployments everywhere from Toyota to Latin American e-commerce giant Mercado Libre. But Unitree is the world leader in terms of volume, dispatching more than 5,500 units in 2025, comprising over a quarter of a global market that is only just waking. In June, Nvidia announced that its bleeding-edge Jetson Thor chip would be fitted into Unitree’s H2 humanoid for sale to research institutions around the globe. 

We could be, Wang says, anything from two to 10 years away from a “ChatGPT moment” for humanoids, whereby a robot achieves sufficient “generalization” to be able to execute 80% of requested voice commands in an 80% unfamiliar environment. “Once the technology surpasses this ... inflection point, robots can gradually be rolled out for large-scale commercial use,” he says.

If the timeline for that breakthrough is a matter of debate, its potential impact is not. Nonunion robots (the only kind) could operate around the clock on assembly lines, replace some nurses in hospitals, and pick fruit under the hot sun. 

The positive outlook is that wide-scale humanoid adoption will herald an unprecedented era of global abundance, automating mundane and dangerous labor while ushering in post-scarcity societies. There is a less rosy divination, however. Amid a mass labor displacement, robots concentrate even more wealth into fewer human hands. With production severed from wages, people could face an identity crisis as robots begin to dominate manual labor and service industries, while governments contend with strained social safety nets and rising competition between the U.S. and China—leaders in humanoids as they are in AI—over raw material and technology. On June 3, three U.S. lawmakers introduced the bipartisan Guard Act, which seeks to ban Chinese robots deemed to be a national-security threat. Unitree was singled out by John Moolenaar, a bill co-sponsor and chairman of the House Select Committee on China, who accused Wang’s firm of benefiting from “generous state subsidies” and threatening to bankrupt American competitors.

With AI, we’ve already seen how the ability of large language models (LLM) to parse huge reams of data also makes them susceptible to hallucinations and unintended consequences, like designing biological weapons. But while an LLM can suggest harm, a robot can enact it, auguring a terrifying new cybersecurity peril in the age of embodied AI. Online scams already siphon off staggering sums, but a hacked humanoid could move the threat from screens into homes, streets, nurseries, and factory floors.

The tension between these visions can be reduced to a choice of myth: Are Wang and his GD01, and indeed their competitors, akin to Luke Skywalker and the faithful, guileless C-3PO—or more like Frankenstein and his monster?

Wang, unsurprisingly, veers toward the positive, predicting that in 10 years every household may have a small robot that can do your laundry, grill your steak, and even provide basic care if you fall ill. They can also take on tasks most humans would rather avoid, from steep mountain farming to sewer maintenance.

But if that sounds like a future arriving faster than society can absorb it, Wang insists the transition will be gradual. “It will evolve step-by-step,” he says. “If society finds that robots don’t perform well in a certain sector, or if people simply don’t like them there, we can easily pivot and re-direct their application to other fields. So, everything remains well within controllable boundaries.”

Wang is an unlikely prophet of the AI age. Slight and bespectacled, he possesses none of the performative bravado associated with many technology founders. Instead, he speaks about robotics with quiet, unwavering conviction.

The possibilities and promises of a humanoid revolution have occupied him since he was 10 years old and first watched early televised robotics experiments performed at MIT by Marc Raibert, founder of Boston Dynamics. He spent his free time building model aircraft, conducting experiments, and, before long, assembling miniature turbojet engines. 

Meet Unitree’s GD01 Robot

In 2009, Wang enrolled at Zhejiang Sci-Tech University in Hangzhou, majoring in mechatronics engineering. As a first-year student, he says, he built his first small bipedal robot, capable of walking a few steps, on less than $30. That same price-conscious approach would underpin Unitree’s flourishing. While pursuing a master’s degree at Shanghai University, he developed a prototype quadruped robot, XDog, which still occupies pride of place in the Unitree headquarters. Departing from the hydraulic-powered designs popularized by his idols at Boston Dynamics, Wang opted for a cost-saving electric-motor model, which helped XDog win second prize in a national entrepreneurship competition.

After graduation and a stint at Shenzhen-based drone trailblazer DJI, Wang launched Unitree with 2 million RMB ($300,000) in capital from an angel investor. It has since become a cultural phenomenon; an estimated 600 million people watched videos of Unitree’s H2 and G1 humanoid robots swinging nunchucks and executing flips as part of February’s Spring Festival Gala—China’s equivalent to the Super Bowl halftime show. It was the second year that Unitree humanoids provided the headline act, and all eyes are on what Wang has planned next time. “I have no idea,” he confesses with a laugh.

In March, Unitree filed for an IPO that would value the firm at $6 billion, sparking a buying frenzy in mainland Chinese markets for companies that would benefit along with it, such as upstream suppliers.

But high expectations are both a blessing and a curse. While Unitree’s first-quarter revenue surged over 68% year-on-year to $62 million, adjusted net profit halved over the same period to just $6 million, which the company blamed on a significant spike in R&D and sales costs. Wang talks about “a flip side” to viral internet fame. “Because the hype gets so intense, it places a fair amount of pressure on our company and the industry as a whole,” he says. “Everyone starts expecting your technology to leap forward at a breakneck pace. But the truth is, deep tech breakthroughs require a certain amount of time.”

Unitree’s blossoming came just as the Chinese government began leaning into AI and robotics, which President Xi Jinping has highlighted as key examples of the “new growth drivers.” China’s Ministry of Industry and Information Technology in turn declared humanoids a “disruptive innovation” on the same level as smartphones and EVs—breakthroughs destined to transform human production and lifestyle, making leadership a matter of economic and national security. It’s a policy push backed by hard cash; since late 2024, Chinese cities have put together investment funds worth over $26 billion, according to Morgan Stanley. Unitree hasn’t disclosed the exact level of state support it receives, though as one of Hangzhou’s “Six Little Dragons”—elite local startups—it benefits from the city’s $14 billion Sci-Tech Fund. “China is an ass kicker, next level,” Elon Musk told a Tesla earnings call in January. “To the best of our knowledge, we don’t see any significant [humanoid robot] competitors outside of China.”Wang’s role leading that charge was evident when he appeared as the youngest member of a high-profile business symposium hosted by Xi last spring. More than 140 Chinese companies now make humanoid robots, yet the perception inside China is that Unitree is not just another robotics startup, but a hardware platform destined to replicate what BYD achieved for EVs and DJI did for drones. Wang’s strategy is simple but hard to beat: first, control the most difficult core components; leverage scale to relentlessly drive down costs; and use each product generation to unlock new markets.

Unitree has slashed the pretax price of its flagship G1 humanoid from $16,000 to $13,500 in just 18 months. (Still, with components that cost under $9,000, according to recent estimates by SemiAnalysis, that gives Unitree a handy 33% gross margin.) The R1 model now retails for under $5,000, and over six years the price of their “robot dogs” has dropped from $45,000 to under $2,000. Wang insists Unitree wins through manufacturing cost efficiency—for example, it produces its own actuators, the “muscles” responsible for movement, which account for 50% to 70% of a humanoid’s cost. 

While the G1 might not boast the peak performance of some Western competitors in terms of load bearing and durability, it has crossed the “good enough” threshold for real-world deployment. With the ability to carry 5-kg payloads for 10 to 15 minutes continuously without overheating, it is moving out of R&D labs and into pilot deployments for warehouse logistics and material handling.

That said, to date only a fraction of humanoids sold are employed in actual useful work. The reason is very simple: today’s robots operate at about 30% to 50% of human efficiency for general factory and warehouse tasks, such as stacking and carrying boxes. Only 9% of Unitree’s sales go directly into industrial applications, with 17% for commercial venues such as tourist sites, exhibitions, and retail stores. That leaves 74% of Unitree’s humanoid sales to universities, research institutions, and individual developers looking to hone their own AI and motion-control capabilities. And the company is far from alone. The entire modern humanoid industry is essentially selling robots to people trying to build better robots. “Humanoid robots are just not ready for the mass market,” says Grace Shao, who publishes the AI Proem newsletter. 

Wang is unconcerned, however, and compares this trend to the early days of the personal computer, when they were primarily purchased by those in the business: “As the software becomes increasingly mature, with more developers joining each year, the proportion of genuine industrial deployments is growing at an incredibly fast pace.”

For now, Unitree is still wrestling with the same white whale as its legion of competitors: the generalization gap, which Wang calls “the biggest headache for the entire global scientific community.” While today’s back-flipping machines are engineering marvels, they remain stubbornly task-specific. If you want a robot to pick up a pen and move it across a desk, you must program every granular motion. But to make AI truly useful in the physical world, robots must transition from executing rigid scripts to understanding novel commands—like being told to simply “go to the grocery store.”

Achieving this milestone will require overcoming two self-reinforcing problems: the scarcity of 3D data and the diversity of deployment environments. Unlike LLMs like ChatGPT, which can parse the virtually boundless internet, robots need manipulation data collected in the real physical world, which is costly and difficult to scale. It must typically be collected manually by repetitively training machines how to perform certain tasks, such as folding shirts, stocking refrigerators, or shaking a mai tai. Plus, explains Jiang Zheyuan, CEO of Beijing-based humanoid firm Noetix, “even if a well-performing manipulation strategy is trained for a specific task, the success rate often drops sharply when the environment changes slightly—for example, object displacement, different lighting, or different tabletop materials.” 

Thanks to firms like Unitree, the hardware for humanoids is advancing quickly. The real battleground is over the robot brains, or vision-language-action (VLA) models, which are designed to turn human inputs into embodied AI performing intuitively and accurately. The VLA space alone will be worth $40.50 billion by 2035, predicts Kaiso Research, a market intelligence firm, and many of tech’s biggest players are aggressively pursuing their own models, including Nvidia’s GR00T N1.7, Google DeepMind’s RT-2-X, and Alibaba’s Qwen Robot Suite.

That battle is increasingly about one thing: collecting data. In New York City, for example, Micro AGI, which counts Unitree as a partner, is sending camera-wearing staff to clean apartments free of charge simply to generate 3D data to train its physical AI model. But as a dedicated robotics company, Unitree is arguably in a weaker position than Tesla, Xiaomi, or XPeng, which have diversified revenue streams and established supply chains. So solving this generalization gap is key to maintaining its status as today’s market champion. After all, its absolute sales are by any measure minuscule, meaning whoever closes the gap first could easily surge ahead.

There may be a game changer on the horizon: revolutionary new 6G connectivity would enable AI-native networks on which collective learning happens seamlessly across millions of connected devices. If one humanoid in a distant warehouse wrestles with a novel tool and finally masters it, that newly generated data can be instantly uploaded and shared across the entire global fleet. 6G will empower us “to make a digital twin of the world,” Cristiano Amon, CEO of $200 billion semiconductor firm Qualcomm, tells TIME. 

Boosted connectivity would solve the robotics bottlenecks of weight and battery life. Navigating our world requires simultaneously processing 4K video, spatial lidar, audio, and delicate tactile or proprioceptive feedback. But carrying heavy computational hardware and the means to power it severely limits both robot agility and operating time. 6G could potentially combine cloud processing with network lag of a near instantaneous 0.1 to 1 millisecond. Suddenly, lightweight physical robots could be animated by a cloud-based AI brain operating in real time. When a robot fumbles, 6G provides a safety net via remote human teleoperation.

As these machines come online, a significant challenge will be securing any centralized network. Last year, cybersecurity analysts discovered a “backdoor” in Unitree’s Go1 robot dogs, whereby third parties could log onto a portal to see the robot’s location, view live camera feeds, and even control it remotely. In response, Unitree shut down the cloud service that enabled the breach. Wang insists his “robot products operate completely disconnected from the network. If the client does not permit it, the robot will not save any environmental information whatsoever.” Besides, he adds, the data stored on a robot is “entirely negligible” when compared with that kept on a smartphone.

Then there is the fear, familiar from fiction, that machines built to act on our behalf might one day be made to act against us. Even today’s robots have shown physical AI carries physical risk: viral clips capture Unitree machines swinging an arm or a leg into unsuspecting children who stray into the path of their choreography. As humanoids graduate to generalization, whether they’ll become more cautious or more dangerous is a key question facing the industry. Wang plays down the potential for renegade robots to wreak havoc. “That won’t happen,” he says. “These robots are engineered with rigorous hardware constraints and safety protections.” 

The need for safety will hit home if human-shaped machines step off assembly lines and into living rooms. On June 30, Shenzhen-based UBtech launched its lifelike U1 humanoids specifically for companionship, featuring silicone skin and biomimetic facial expressions, and powered by what the company claims is world’s first empathic LLM “capable of recognizing more than 20 fine-grained emotional states.”

But the greatest obstacle to the household humanoid may not be technical. First proposed by Japanese roboticist Masahiro Mori in 1970, the “uncanny valley” hypothesis posits that as an object becomes more humanlike, our affection for it grows. Once it crosses a critical threshold—getting too close to human—our response plunges into revulsion. As firms experiment with anthropomorphic robots with real hair, it remains to be seen whether the valley can be bridged.

At Unitree’s headquarters, the GD01 was displayed hanging from a reinforced steel joist. Wang was unwilling to operate it or even climb inside. “It might not be safe,” he shrugs of his creation, which carries a retail price of $650,000. When TIME’s photographer asked for a nearby promotional background to be relocated, Unitree’s technicians protested that shifting it would interfere with the lidar of the nearby phalanx of G1s practicing that now iconic Spring Festival Gala acrobatic martial-arts display. When the performance finished, those technicians rushed to spray their now sizzling lidar with coolant following that four-minute exertion.

For all Unitree’s lead in the humanoid market, today’s machines remain strangely theatrical: bodies dressed as intelligence, rehearsing a role they have not yet learned to live. “Humanoid robot technology is still in its relatively early stages,” cautions Wang. “It will likely take some time before we see genuine, large-scale deployment. It won’t happen overnight.” Nearby, GD01—part prototype, part prophecy—stands sentinel, as if quietly rebuking his master’s prudence.

Last year, cybersecurity analysts discovered a “backdoor” in Unitree’s Go1 robot dogs, whereby third parties could log onto a portal to see the robot’s location, view live camera feeds, and even control it remotely. In response, Unitree shut down the cloud service that enabled the breach. Wang insists his “robot products operate completely disconnected from the network. If the client does not permit it, the robot will not save any environmental information whatsoever.” Besides, he adds, the data stored on a robot is “entirely negligible” when compared with that kept on a smartphone.Then there is the fear, familiar from fiction, that machines built to act on our behalf might one day be made to act against us. Even today’s robots have shown physical AI carries physical risk: viral clips capture Unitree machines swinging an arm or a leg into unsuspecting children who stray into the path of their choreography. As humanoids graduate to generalization, whether they’ll become more cautious or more dangerous is a key question facing the industry. Wang plays down the potential for renegade robots to wreak havoc. “That won’t happen,” he says. “These robots are engineered with rigorous hardware constraints and safety protections.” The need for safety will hit home if human-shaped machines step off assembly lines and into living rooms. On June 30, Shenzhen-based UBtech launched its lifelike U1 humanoids specifically for companionship, featuring silicone skin and biomimetic facial expressions, and powered by what the company claims is world’s first empathic LLM “capable of recognizing more than 20 fine-grained emotional states.”But the greatest obstacle to the household humanoid may not be technical. First proposed by Japanese roboticist Masahiro Mori in 1970, the “uncanny valley” hypothesis posits that as an object becomes more humanlike, our affection for it grows. Once it crosses a critical threshold—getting too close to human—our response plunges into revulsion. As firms experiment with anthropomorphic robots with real hair, it remains to be seen whether the valley can be bridged.At Unitree’s headquarters, the GD01 was displayed hanging from a reinforced steel joist. Wang was unwilling to operate it or even climb inside. “It might not be safe,” he shrugs of his creation, which carries a retail price of $650,000. When TIME’s photographer asked for a nearby promotional background to be relocated, Unitree’s technicians protested that shifting it would interfere with the lidar of the nearby phalanx of G1s practicing that now iconic Spring Festival Gala acrobatic martial-arts display. When the performance finished, those technicians rushed to spray their now sizzling lidar with coolant following that four-minute exertion.For all Unitree’s lead in the humanoid market, today’s machines remain strangely theatrical: bodies dressed as intelligence, rehearsing a role they have not yet learned to live. “Humanoid robot technology is still in its relatively early stages,” cautions Wang. “It will likely take some time before we see genuine, large-scale deployment. It won’t happen overnight.” Nearby, GD01—part prototype, part prophecy—stands sentinel, as if quietly rebuking his master’s prudence.

blocks.html_en: Wang has done more than arguably anyone else to bring this science fiction closer to reality. A native of China’s coastal city of Ningbo, he founded Unitree in 2016 when he was just 26 years old and has moved from robot dogs to humanoid robots and now, with GD01, human mobility machines. But while the GD01 might inspire awe, humbler humanoids like Unitree’s flagship G1 are the ones set to become the workhorses of a global robotics transformation already gathering pace in China, the U.S., and beyond.

blocks.html_en: This is the next great tech frontier, one that has previously been the purview of Star Wars and Blade Runner: AI that walks, sees, hears and physically interacts with the real world. Morgan Stanley predicts that as many as 13 million humanoids could walk among us by 2035—equivalent to the population of Bolivia. That number is forecast to rise to 1 billion by 2050, when the industry could be worth $5 trillion.

blocks.html_en: Unitree is hardly alone staking a claim in this space. In January, Tesla announced the third generation of its Optimus humanoid robot with a goal to eventually produce 10 million units per year at factories in Fremont and Texas. In late June, California-based Figure dispatched its latest 03 humanoid to BMW’s Spartanburg plant in South Carolina, where its earlier 02 model helped assemble 30,000 cars last year. Oregon-based Agility, meanwhile, has secured deployments everywhere from Toyota to Latin American e-commerce giant Mercado Libre. But Unitree is the world leader in terms of volume, dispatching more than 5,500 units in 2025, comprising over a quarter of a global market that is only just waking. In June, Nvidia announced that its bleeding-edge Jetson Thor chip would be fitted into Unitree’s H2 humanoid for sale to research institutions around the globe.

blocks.html_en: The positive outlook is that wide-scale humanoid adoption will herald an unprecedented era of global abundance, automating mundane and dangerous labor while ushering in post-scarcity societies. There is a less rosy divination, however. Amid a mass labor displacement, robots concentrate even more wealth into fewer human hands. With production severed from wages, people could face an identity crisis as robots begin to dominate manual labor and service industries, while governments contend with strained social safety nets and rising competition between the U.S. and China—leaders in humanoids as they are in AI—over raw material and technology. On June 3, three U.S. lawmakers introduced the bipartisan Guard Act, which seeks to ban Chinese robots deemed to be a national-security threat. Unitree was singled out by John Moolenaar, a bill co-sponsor and chairman of the House Select Committee on China, who accused Wang’s firm of benefiting from “generous state subsidies” and threatening to bankrupt American competitors.

blocks.html_en: With AI, we’ve already seen how the ability of large language models (LLM) to parse huge reams of data also makes them susceptible to hallucinations and unintended consequences, like designing biological weapons. But while an LLM can suggest harm, a robot can enact it, auguring a terrifying new cybersecurity peril in the age of embodied AI. Online scams already siphon off staggering sums, but a hacked humanoid could move the threat from screens into homes, streets, nurseries, and factory floors.

So, everything remains well within controllable boundaries.”

blocks.html_en: In 2009, Wang enrolled at Zhejiang Sci-Tech University in Hangzhou, majoring in mechatronics engineering. As a first-year student, he says, he built his first small bipedal robot, capable of walking a few steps, on less than $30. That same price-conscious approach would underpin Unitree’s flourishing. While pursuing a master’s degree at Shanghai University, he developed a prototype quadruped robot, XDog, which still occupies pride of place in the Unitree headquarters. Departing from the hydraulic-powered designs popularized by his idols at Boston Dynamics, Wang opted for a cost-saving electric-motor model, which helped XDog win second prize in a national entrepreneurship competition.

sentences.en: That same price-conscious approach would underpin Unitree’s flourishing.
sentences.zh: 同样的价格意识策略也支撑了 Unitree 的蓬勃发展。

sentences.en: While pursuing a master’s degree at Shanghai University, he developed a prototype quadruped robot, XDog, which still occupies pride of place in the Unitree headquarters.
sentences.zh: 在上海大学攻读硕士学位期间，他开发了一款四足机器人原型 XDog，至今仍在 Unitree 总部占据一席之地。

sentences.en: Departing from the hydraulic-powered designs popularized by his idols at Boston Dynamics, Wang opted for a cost-saving electric-motor model, which helped XDog win second prize in a national entrepreneurship competition.
sentences.zh: 与他在波士顿动力公司的偶像所流行的液压动力设计不同，王选择了节省成本的电动机模型，这帮助XDog在全国创业大赛中获得了二等奖。

blocks.html_en: After graduation and a stint at Shenzhen-based drone trailblazer DJI, Wang launched Unitree with 2 million RMB ($300,000) in capital from an angel investor. It has since become a cultural phenomenon; an estimated 600 million people watched videos of Unitree’s H2 and G1 humanoid robots swinging nunchucks and executing flips as part of February’s Spring Festival Gala—China’s equivalent to the Super Bowl halftime show. It was the second year that Unitree humanoids provided the headline act, and all eyes are on what Wang has planned next time. “I have no idea,” he confesses with a laugh.

sentences.en: After graduation and a stint at Shenzhen-based drone trailblazer DJI, Wang launched Unitree with 2 million RMB ($300,000) in capital from an angel investor.
sentences.zh: 毕业并在深圳无人机先驱大疆创新工作一段时间后，王从一位天使投资人那里获得了 200 万元人民币（30 万美元）的资金，创立了 Unitree。

sentences.en: It has since become a cultural phenomenon; an estimated 600 million people watched videos of Unitree’s H2 and G1 humanoid robots swinging nunchucks and executing flips as part of February’s Spring Festival Gala—China’s equivalent to the Super Bowl halftime show.
sentences.zh: 从那时起，它已成为一种文化现象；据估计，有 6 亿人观看了 Unitree 的 H2 和 G1 人形机器人在 2 月份的春节联欢晚会（相当于中国的超级碗中场秀）中挥舞双节棍和执行翻转的视频。

sentences.en: It was the second year that Unitree humanoids provided the headline act, and all eyes are on what Wang has planned next time.
sentences.zh: 这是 Unitree 类人机器人第二年成为头条新闻，所有的目光都集中在王下一次的计划上。

sentences.en: “I have no idea,” he confesses with a laugh.
sentences.zh: “我不知道，”他笑着承认。

blocks.html_en: In March, Unitree filed for an IPO that would value the firm at $6 billion, sparking a buying frenzy in mainland Chinese markets for companies that would benefit along with it, such as upstream suppliers.

sentences.en: In March, Unitree filed for an IPO that would value the firm at $6 billion, sparking a buying frenzy in mainland Chinese markets for companies that would benefit along with it, such as upstream suppliers.
sentences.zh: 今年 3 月，Unitree 申请首次公开募股，该公司的估值将达到 60 亿美元，在中国大陆市场引发了上游供应商等受益公司的收购狂潮。

blocks.html_en: But high expectations are both a blessing and a curse. While Unitree’s first-quarter revenue surged over 68% year-on-year to $62 million, adjusted net profit halved over the same period to just $6 million, which the company blamed on a significant spike in R&D and sales costs. Wang talks about “a flip side” to viral internet fame. “Because the hype gets so intense, it places a fair amount of pressure on our company and the industry as a whole,” he says. “Everyone starts expecting your technology to leap forward at a breakneck pace. But the truth is, deep tech breakthroughs require a certain amount of time.”

sentences.en: But high expectations are both a blessing and a curse.
sentences.zh: 但过高的期望既是福也是祸。

sentences.en: While Unitree’s first-quarter revenue surged over 68% year-on-year to $62 million, adjusted net profit halved over the same period to just $6 million, which the company blamed on a significant spike in R&D and sales costs.
sentences.zh: 虽然Unitree第一季度收入同比飙升超过68%，达到6200万美元，但同期调整后净利润却减少了一半，仅为600万美元，该公司将其归咎于研发和销售成本大幅上涨。

sentences.en: Wang talks about “a flip side” to viral internet fame.
sentences.zh: 王谈到了网络病毒式传播的“另一面”。

sentences.en: “Because the hype gets so intense, it places a fair amount of pressure on our company and the industry as a whole,” he says.
sentences.zh: “由于炒作如此激烈，这给我们公司和整个行业带来了相当大的压力，”他说。

sentences.en: “Everyone starts expecting your technology to leap forward at a breakneck pace.
sentences.zh: “每个人都开始期待你的技术以惊人的速度飞跃发展。

sentences.en: But the truth is, deep tech breakthroughs require a certain amount of time.”
sentences.zh: 但事实是，深度技术突破需要一定的时间。”

blocks.html_en: Unitree’s blossoming came just as the Chinese government began leaning into AI and robotics, which President Xi Jinping has highlighted as key examples of the “new growth drivers.” China’s Ministry of Industry and Information Technology in turn declared humanoids a “disruptive innovation” on the same level as smartphones and EVs—breakthroughs destined to transform human production and lifestyle, making leadership a matter of economic and national security. It’s a policy push backed by hard cash; since late 2024, Chinese cities have put together investment funds worth over $26 billion, according to Morgan Stanley. Unitree hasn’t disclosed the exact level of state support it receives, though as one of Hangzhou’s “Six Little Dragons”—elite local startups—it benefits from the city’s $14 billion Sci-Tech Fund. “China is an ass kicker, next level,” Elon Musk told a Tesla earnings call in January. “To the best of our knowledge, we don’t see any significant [humanoid robot] competitors outside of China.”Wang’s role leading that charge was evident when he appeared as the youngest member of a high-profile business symposium hosted by Xi last spring. More than 140 Chinese companies now make humanoid robots, yet the perception inside China is that Unitree is not just another robotics startup, but a hardware platform destined to replicate what BYD achieved for EVs and DJI did for drones. Wang’s strategy is simple but hard to beat: first, control the most difficult core components; leverage scale to relentlessly drive down costs; and use each product generation to unlock new markets.

sentences.en: Unitree’s blossoming came just as the Chinese government began leaning into AI and robotics, which President Xi Jinping has highlighted as key examples of the “new growth drivers.”
sentences.zh: Unitree 的蓬勃发展正值中国政府开始向人工智能和机器人技术倾斜之际，习近平主席强调人工智能和机器人技术是“新增长动力”的关键例子。

sentences.en: China’s Ministry of Industry and Information Technology in turn declared humanoids a “disruptive innovation” on the same level as smartphones and EVs—breakthroughs destined to transform human production and lifestyle, making leadership a matter of economic and national security.
sentences.zh: 中国工业和信息化部随后宣布，类人机器人是与智能手机和电动汽车同等水平的“颠覆性创新”，这些突破注定会改变人类的生产和生活方式，使领导力成为经济和国家安全的问题。

sentences.en: It’s a policy push backed by hard cash; since late 2024, Chinese cities have put together investment funds worth over $26 billion, according to Morgan Stanley.
sentences.zh: 这是一项由现金支持的政策推动；据摩根士丹利称，自 2024 年底以来，中国城市已筹集了价值超过 260 亿美元的投资基金。

sentences.en: Unitree hasn’t disclosed the exact level of state support it receives, though as one of Hangzhou’s “Six Little Dragons”—elite local startups—it benefits from the city’s $14 billion Sci-Tech Fund.
sentences.zh: Unitree尚未透露其获得的国家支持的具体水平，但作为杭州“六小龙”（本地精英初创企业）之一，它受益于该市140亿美元的科技基金。

sentences.en: “China is an ass kicker, next level,” Elon Musk told a Tesla earnings call in January.
sentences.zh: 埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 在 1 月份的特斯拉财报电话会议上表示：“中国是一个令人兴奋的国家。”

sentences.en: “To the best of our knowledge, we don’t see any significant [humanoid robot] competitors outside of China.”Wang’s role leading that charge was evident when he appeared as the youngest member of a high-profile business symposium hosted by Xi last spring.
sentences.zh: “据我们所知，我们在中国以外没有看到任何重要的[人形机器人]竞争对手。”去年春天，当王作为最年轻的成员出现在习近平主办的一场备受瞩目的商业研讨会上时，他在这一领域的领导作用就显而易见了。

sentences.en: More than 140 Chinese companies now make humanoid robots, yet the perception inside China is that Unitree is not just another robotics startup, but a hardware platform destined to replicate what BYD achieved for EVs and DJI did for drones.
sentences.zh: 现在有超过 140 家中国公司生产人形机器人，但中国内部的看法是 Unitree 不仅仅是另一家机器人初创公司，而且是一个硬件平台，注定要复制比亚迪在电动汽车方面取得的成就和大疆在无人机方面取得的成就。

sentences.en: Wang’s strategy is simple but hard to beat: first, control the most difficult core components; leverage scale to relentlessly drive down costs; and use each product generation to unlock new markets.
sentences.zh: 王的策略很简单，但很难被击败：首先，控制最难的核心部件；利用规模不断降低成本；并利用每一代产品来开拓新市场。

blocks.html_en: Unitree has slashed the pretax price of its flagship G1 humanoid from $16,000 to $13,500 in just 18 months. (Still, with components that cost under $9,000, according to recent estimates by SemiAnalysis, that gives Unitree a handy 33% gross margin.) The R1 model now retails for under $5,000, and over six years the price of their “robot dogs” has dropped from $45,000 to under $2,000. Wang insists Unitree wins through manufacturing cost efficiency—for example, it produces its own actuators, the “muscles” responsible for movement, which account for 50% to 70% of a humanoid’s cost.

sentences.zh: Unitree 在短短 18 个月内将其旗舰产品 G1 人形机器人的税前价格从 16,000 美元削减至 13,500 美元。

sentences.en: (Still, with components that cost under $9,000, according to recent estimates by SemiAnalysis, that gives Unitree a handy 33% gross margin.)
sentences.zh: （不过，根据 SemiAnalysis 最近的估计，由于组件成本低于 9,000 美元，Unitree 的毛利率高达 33%。）

sentences.zh: R1 型号目前的零售价低于 5,000 美元，六年来，他们的“机器狗”的价格已从 45,000 美元降至 2,000 美元以下。

sentences.zh: Wang 坚持认为 Unitree 通过制造成本效率取胜，例如，它生产自己的执行器，即负责运动的“肌肉”，占人形机器人成本的 50% 至 70%。

blocks.html_en: While the G1 might not boast the peak performance of some Western competitors in terms of load bearing and durability, it has crossed the “good enough” threshold for real-world deployment. With the ability to carry 5-kg payloads for 10 to 15 minutes continuously without overheating, it is moving out of R&D labs and into pilot deployments for warehouse logistics and material handling.

sentences.en: While the G1 might not boast the peak performance of some Western competitors in terms of load bearing and durability, it has crossed the “good enough” threshold for real-world deployment.
sentences.zh: 虽然 G1 在承载和耐用性方面可能无法达到某些西方竞争对手的巅峰性能，但它已经跨越了实际部署的“足够好”门槛。

sentences.en: With the ability to carry 5-kg payloads for 10 to 15 minutes continuously without overheating, it is moving out of R&D labs and into pilot deployments for warehouse logistics and material handling.
sentences.zh: 它能够连续承载 5 公斤有效负载 10 至 15 分钟而不会过热，因此正在走出研发实验室，进入仓库物流和物料搬运的试点部署。

blocks.html_en: That said, to date only a fraction of humanoids sold are employed in actual useful work. The reason is very simple: today’s robots operate at about 30% to 50% of human efficiency for general factory and warehouse tasks, such as stacking and carrying boxes. Only 9% of Unitree’s sales go directly into industrial applications, with 17% for commercial venues such as tourist sites, exhibitions, and retail stores. That leaves 74% of Unitree’s humanoid sales to universities, research institutions, and individual developers looking to hone their own AI and motion-control capabilities. And the company is far from alone. The entire modern humanoid industry is essentially selling robots to people trying to build better robots. “Humanoid robots are just not ready for the mass market,” says Grace Shao, who publishes the AI Proem newsletter.

sentences.en: That said, to date only a fraction of humanoids sold are employed in actual useful work.
sentences.zh: 也就是说，迄今为止，售出的人形机器人中只有一小部分用于实际有用的工作。

sentences.en: That leaves 74% of Unitree’s humanoid sales to universities, research institutions, and individual developers looking to hone their own AI and motion-control capabilities.
sentences.zh: 这使得 Unitree 74% 的人形机器人销售给了大学、研究机构和希望磨练自己的人工智能和运动控制能力的个人开发者。

sentences.zh: 整个现代人形机器人行业本质上是在向试图制造更好机器人的人们出售机器人。

sentences.zh: AI Proem 时事通讯的出版者 Grace Shao 表示：“人形机器人还没有做好进入大众市场的准备。”

blocks.html_en: Wang is unconcerned, however, and compares this trend to the early days of the personal computer, when they were primarily purchased by those in the business: “As the software becomes increasingly mature, with more developers joining each year, the proportion of genuine industrial deployments is growing at an incredibly fast pace.”

sentences.en: Wang is unconcerned, however, and compares this trend to the early days of the personal computer, when they were primarily purchased by those in the business: “As the software becomes increasingly mature, with more developers joining each year, the proportion of genuine industrial deployments is growing at an incredibly fast pace.”
sentences.zh: 然而，王并不担心，他将这一趋势与个人电脑的早期阶段进行了比较，当时个人电脑主要由企业人士购买：“随着软件变得越来越成熟，每年都有更多的开发人员加入，真正的工业部署比例正在以令人难以置信的速度增长。”

blocks.html_en: For now, Unitree is still wrestling with the same white whale as its legion of competitors: the generalization gap, which Wang calls “the biggest headache for the entire global scientific community.” While today’s back-flipping machines are engineering marvels, they remain stubbornly task-specific. If you want a robot to pick up a pen and move it across a desk, you must program every granular motion. But to make AI truly useful in the physical world, robots must transition from executing rigid scripts to understanding novel commands—like being told to simply “go to the grocery store.”

sentences.en: For now, Unitree is still wrestling with the same white whale as its legion of competitors: the generalization gap, which Wang calls “the biggest headache for the entire global scientific community.”
sentences.zh: 目前，Unitree 仍在与众多竞争对手一样面临同样的白鲸：泛化差距，王称这是“整个全球科学界最头疼的问题”。

sentences.en: While today’s back-flipping machines are engineering marvels, they remain stubbornly task-specific.
sentences.zh: 尽管当今的后空翻机器堪称工程奇迹，但它们仍然顽固地针对特定任务。

sentences.en: If you want a robot to pick up a pen and move it across a desk, you must program every granular motion.
sentences.zh: 如果你想让机器人拿起一支笔并在桌子上移动它，你必须对每一个细微的动作进行编程。

sentences.en: But to make AI truly useful in the physical world, robots must transition from executing rigid scripts to understanding novel commands—like being told to simply “go to the grocery store.”
sentences.zh: 但为了让人工智能在现实世界中真正发挥作用，机器人必须从执行严格的脚本转变为理解新颖的命令——比如被告知“去杂货店”。

blocks.html_en: Achieving this milestone will require overcoming two self-reinforcing problems: the scarcity of 3D data and the diversity of deployment environments. Unlike LLMs like ChatGPT, which can parse the virtually boundless internet, robots need manipulation data collected in the real physical world, which is costly and difficult to scale. It must typically be collected manually by repetitively training machines how to perform certain tasks, such as folding shirts, stocking refrigerators, or shaking a mai tai. Plus, explains Jiang Zheyuan, CEO of Beijing-based humanoid firm Noetix, “even if a well-performing manipulation strategy is trained for a specific task, the success rate often drops sharply when the environment changes slightly—for example, object displacement, different lighting, or different tabletop materials.”

sentences.en: Achieving this milestone will require overcoming two self-reinforcing problems: the scarcity of 3D data and the diversity of deployment environments.
sentences.zh: 实现这一里程碑需要克服两个自我强化的问题：3D 数据的稀缺性和部署环境的多样性。

sentences.en: Unlike LLMs like ChatGPT, which can parse the virtually boundless internet, robots need manipulation data collected in the real physical world, which is costly and difficult to scale.
sentences.zh: 与 ChatGPT 等可以解析几乎无限的互联网的法学硕士不同，机器人需要在现实物理世界中收集的操作数据，这是昂贵且难以扩展的。

sentences.en: It must typically be collected manually by repetitively training machines how to perform certain tasks, such as folding shirts, stocking refrigerators, or shaking a mai tai.
sentences.zh: 通常必须通过重复训练机器如何执行某些任务来手动收集它，例如折叠衬衫、给冰箱存货或摇晃麦泰酒。

sentences.en: Plus, explains Jiang Zheyuan, CEO of Beijing-based humanoid firm Noetix, “even if a well-performing manipulation strategy is trained for a specific task, the success rate often drops sharply when the environment changes slightly—for example, object displacement, different lighting, or different tabletop materials.”
sentences.zh: 另外，总部位于北京的人形机器人公司Noetix的首席执行官姜哲元解释说，“即使针对特定任务训练了性能良好的操纵策略，当环境发生轻微变化时，例如物体位移、不同的照明或不同的桌面材料，成功率往往会急剧下降。”

blocks.html_en: Thanks to firms like Unitree, the hardware for humanoids is advancing quickly. The real battleground is over the robot brains, or vision-language-action (VLA) models, which are designed to turn human inputs into embodied AI performing intuitively and accurately. The VLA space alone will be worth $40.50 billion by 2035, predicts Kaiso Research, a market intelligence firm, and many of tech’s biggest players are aggressively pursuing their own models, including Nvidia’s GR00T N1.7, Google DeepMind’s RT-2-X, and Alibaba’s Qwen Robot Suite.

sentences.en: Thanks to firms like Unitree, the hardware for humanoids is advancing quickly.
sentences.zh: 得益于像 Unitree 这样的公司，类人机器人的硬件正在快速发展。

sentences.en: The real battleground is over the robot brains, or vision-language-action (VLA) models, which are designed to turn human inputs into embodied AI performing intuitively and accurately.
sentences.zh: 真正的战场是机器人大脑或视觉语言动作（VLA）模型，这些模型旨在将人类输入转化为直观而准确地执行的具体人工智能。

sentences.en: The VLA space alone will be worth $40.50 billion by 2035, predicts Kaiso Research, a market intelligence firm, and many of tech’s biggest players are aggressively pursuing their own models, including Nvidia’s GR00T N1.7, Google DeepMind’s RT-2-X, and Alibaba’s Qwen Robot Suite.
sentences.zh: 市场情报公司 Kaiso Research 预测，到 2035 年，仅 VLA 领域的价值就将达到 405 亿美元，许多科技巨头都在积极开发自己的模型，包括 Nvidia 的 GR00T N1.7、Google DeepMind 的 RT-2-X 和阿里巴巴的 Qwen Robot Suite。

blocks.html_en: That battle is increasingly about one thing: collecting data. In New York City, for example, Micro AGI, which counts Unitree as a partner, is sending camera-wearing staff to clean apartments free of charge simply to generate 3D data to train its physical AI model. But as a dedicated robotics company, Unitree is arguably in a weaker position than Tesla, Xiaomi, or XPeng, which have diversified revenue streams and established supply chains. So solving this generalization gap is key to maintaining its status as today’s market champion. After all, its absolute sales are by any measure minuscule, meaning whoever closes the gap first could easily surge ahead.

sentences.en: That battle is increasingly about one thing: collecting data.
sentences.zh: 这场战斗越来越关注一件事：收集数据。

sentences.en: In New York City, for example, Micro AGI, which counts Unitree as a partner, is sending camera-wearing staff to clean apartments free of charge simply to generate 3D data to train its physical AI model.
sentences.zh: 例如，在纽约市，Unitree 作为合作伙伴的 Micro AGI 正在派遣佩戴摄像头的工作人员免费清洁公寓，只是为了生成 3D 数据来训练其物理 AI 模型。

sentences.en: But as a dedicated robotics company, Unitree is arguably in a weaker position than Tesla, Xiaomi, or XPeng, which have diversified revenue streams and established supply chains.
sentences.zh: 但作为一家专注的机器人公司，Unitree 的地位可以说比特斯拉、小米或小鹏汽车处于弱势，这些公司拥有多元化的收入来源并建立了供应链。

sentences.en: So solving this generalization gap is key to maintaining its status as today’s market champion.
sentences.zh: 因此，解决这一普遍性差距是维持其当今市场冠军地位的关键。

sentences.en: After all, its absolute sales are by any measure minuscule, meaning whoever closes the gap first could easily surge ahead.
sentences.zh: 毕竟，无论以何种标准衡量，其绝对销售额都微乎其微，这意味着无论谁先缩小差距，都可以轻松领先。

blocks.html_en: There may be a game changer on the horizon: revolutionary new 6G connectivity would enable AI-native networks on which collective learning happens seamlessly across millions of connected devices. If one humanoid in a distant warehouse wrestles with a novel tool and finally masters it, that newly generated data can be instantly uploaded and shared across the entire global fleet. 6G will empower us “to make a digital twin of the world,” Cristiano Amon, CEO of $200 billion semiconductor firm Qualcomm, tells TIME.

sentences.en: There may be a game changer on the horizon: revolutionary new 6G connectivity would enable AI-native networks on which collective learning happens seamlessly across millions of connected devices.
sentences.zh: 游戏规则的改变可能即将到来：革命性的新 6G 连接将使人工智能原生网络成为可能，集体学习可以在数百万台联网设备上无缝进行。

sentences.en: If one humanoid in a distant warehouse wrestles with a novel tool and finally masters it, that newly generated data can be instantly uploaded and shared across the entire global fleet.
sentences.zh: 如果遥远仓库中的一个人形机器人努力研究一种新颖的工具并最终掌握它，那么新生成的数据可以立即上传并在整个全球舰队中共享。

sentences.en: 6G will empower us “to make a digital twin of the world,” Cristiano Amon, CEO of $200 billion semiconductor firm Qualcomm, tells TIME.
sentences.zh: 市值 2000 亿美元的半导体公司高通首席执行官克里斯蒂亚诺·阿蒙 (Cristiano Amon) 告诉《时代》杂志，6G 将使我们能够“打造世界的数字孪生”。

blocks.html_en: Boosted connectivity would solve the robotics bottlenecks of weight and battery life. Navigating our world requires simultaneously processing 4K video, spatial lidar, audio, and delicate tactile or proprioceptive feedback. But carrying heavy computational hardware and the means to power it severely limits both robot agility and operating time. 6G could potentially combine cloud processing with network lag of a near instantaneous 0.1 to 1 millisecond. Suddenly, lightweight physical robots could be animated by a cloud-based AI brain operating in real time. When a robot fumbles, 6G provides a safety net via remote human teleoperation.

sentences.en: Boosted connectivity would solve the robotics bottlenecks of weight and battery life.
sentences.zh: 增强的连接性将解决机器人的重量和电池寿命瓶颈。

sentences.en: Navigating our world requires simultaneously processing 4K video, spatial lidar, audio, and delicate tactile or proprioceptive feedback.
sentences.zh: 探索我们的世界需要同时处理 4K 视频、空间激光雷达、音频和微妙的触觉或本体感受反馈。

sentences.en: But carrying heavy computational hardware and the means to power it severely limits both robot agility and operating time.
sentences.zh: 但携带重型计算硬件及其供电方式严重限制了机器人的敏捷性和操作时间。

sentences.en: 6G could potentially combine cloud processing with network lag of a near instantaneous 0.1 to 1 millisecond.
sentences.zh: 6G 可能会将云处理与近乎瞬时的 0.1 至 1 毫秒的网络延迟结合起来。

sentences.en: Suddenly, lightweight physical robots could be animated by a cloud-based AI brain operating in real time.
sentences.zh: 突然间，轻型物理机器人可以通过实时运行的基于云的人工智能大脑进行动画处理。

sentences.en: When a robot fumbles, 6G provides a safety net via remote human teleoperation.
sentences.zh: 当机器人失手时，6G 通过远程人工遥控操作提供安全网。

blocks.html_en: As these machines come online, a significant challenge will be securing any centralized network. Last year, cybersecurity analysts discovered a “backdoor” in Unitree’s Go1 robot dogs, whereby third parties could log onto a portal to see the robot’s location, view live camera feeds, and even control it remotely. In response, Unitree shut down the cloud service that enabled the breach. Wang insists his “robot products operate completely disconnected from the network. If the client does not permit it, the robot will not save any environmental information whatsoever.” Besides, he adds, the data stored on a robot is “entirely negligible” when compared with that kept on a smartphone.

sentences.en: As these machines come online, a significant challenge will be securing any centralized network.
sentences.zh: 随着这些机器上线，一个重大挑战将是保护任何集中式网络。

sentences.en: Last year, cybersecurity analysts discovered a “backdoor” in Unitree’s Go1 robot dogs, whereby third parties could log onto a portal to see the robot’s location, view live camera feeds, and even control it remotely.
sentences.zh: 去年，网络安全分析师在 Unitree 的 Go1 机器狗中发现了一个“后门”，第三方可以通过该后门登录门户来查看机器人的位置、查看实时摄像头信息，甚至远程控制它。

sentences.en: In response, Unitree shut down the cloud service that enabled the breach.
sentences.zh: 作为回应，Unitree 关闭了导致漏洞的云服务。

sentences.en: Wang insists his “robot products operate completely disconnected from the network.
sentences.zh: 王坚称他的“机器人产品完全脱离网络运行。

sentences.en: If the client does not permit it, the robot will not save any environmental information whatsoever.”
sentences.zh: 如果客户不允许，机器人将不会保存任何环境信息。”

sentences.en: Besides, he adds, the data stored on a robot is “entirely negligible” when compared with that kept on a smartphone.
sentences.zh: 此外，他补充说，与智能手机上保存的数据相比，机器人上存储的数据“完全可以忽略不计”。

blocks.html_en: Then there is the fear, familiar from fiction, that machines built to act on our behalf might one day be made to act against us. Even today’s robots have shown physical AI carries physical risk: viral clips capture Unitree machines swinging an arm or a leg into unsuspecting children who stray into the path of their choreography. As humanoids graduate to generalization, whether they’ll become more cautious or more dangerous is a key question facing the industry. Wang plays down the potential for renegade robots to wreak havoc. “That won’t happen,” he says. “These robots are engineered with rigorous hardware constraints and safety protections.”

sentences.en: Then there is the fear, familiar from fiction, that machines built to act on our behalf might one day be made to act against us.
sentences.zh: 还有一种恐惧，就像小说中常见的那样，为我们而制造的机器有一天可能会针对我们而行动。

sentences.en: Even today’s robots have shown physical AI carries physical risk: viral clips capture Unitree machines swinging an arm or a leg into unsuspecting children who stray into the path of their choreography.
sentences.zh: 即使是今天的机器人也已经表明，物理人工智能也存在物理风险：病毒剪辑捕捉到 Unitree 机器向毫无戒心的孩子挥动手臂或腿，导致他们误入编排的路径。

sentences.en: As humanoids graduate to generalization, whether they’ll become more cautious or more dangerous is a key question facing the industry.
sentences.zh: 随着类人机器人逐渐走向通用化，它们是否会变得更加谨慎或更加危险是该行业面临的一个关键问题。

sentences.en: Wang plays down the potential for renegade robots to wreak havoc.
sentences.zh: 王淡化了叛逆机器人造成严重破坏的可能性。

sentences.en: “That won’t happen,” he says.
sentences.zh: “那不会发生，”他说。

sentences.en: “These robots are engineered with rigorous hardware constraints and safety protections.”
sentences.zh: “这些机器人的设计具有严格的硬件限制和安全保护。”

blocks.html_en: The need for safety will hit home if human-shaped machines step off assembly lines and into living rooms. On June 30, Shenzhen-based UBtech launched its lifelike U1 humanoids specifically for companionship, featuring silicone skin and biomimetic facial expressions, and powered by what the company claims is world’s first empathic LLM “capable of recognizing more than 20 fine-grained emotional states.”

sentences.en: The need for safety will hit home if human-shaped machines step off assembly lines and into living rooms.
sentences.zh: 如果人形机器走下装配线并进入客厅，对安全的需求就会直击要害。

sentences.en: On June 30, Shenzhen-based UBtech launched its lifelike U1 humanoids specifically for companionship, featuring silicone skin and biomimetic facial expressions, and powered by what the company claims is world’s first empathic LLM “capable of recognizing more than 20 fine-grained emotional states.”
sentences.zh: 6 月 30 日，总部位于深圳的 UBtech 推出了专门用于陪伴的逼真 U1 人形机器人，具有硅胶皮肤和仿生面部表情，并由该公司声称的世界上第一个具有同理心的法学硕士提供支持，“能够识别 20 多种细粒度的情绪状态”。

blocks.html_en: But the greatest obstacle to the household humanoid may not be technical. First proposed by Japanese roboticist Masahiro Mori in 1970, the “uncanny valley” hypothesis posits that as an object becomes more humanlike, our affection for it grows. Once it crosses a critical threshold—getting too close to human—our response plunges into revulsion. As firms experiment with anthropomorphic robots with real hair, it remains to be seen whether the valley can be bridged.

sentences.en: But the greatest obstacle to the household humanoid may not be technical.
sentences.zh: 但家庭人形机器人的最大障碍可能不是技术。

sentences.en: First proposed by Japanese roboticist Masahiro Mori in 1970, the “uncanny valley” hypothesis posits that as an object becomes more humanlike, our affection for it grows.
sentences.zh: “恐怖谷”假说由日本机器人学家 Masahiro Mori 于 1970 年首次提出，它认为，当一个物体变得越来越像人类时，我们对它的喜爱就会增加。

sentences.en: Once it crosses a critical threshold—getting too close to human—our response plunges into revulsion.
sentences.zh: 一旦它跨过一个临界阈值——太接近人类——我们的反应就会陷入厌恶。

sentences.en: As firms experiment with anthropomorphic robots with real hair, it remains to be seen whether the valley can be bridged.
sentences.zh: 当各家公司尝试用真发拟人化机器人时，这个山谷能否弥合还有待观察。

blocks.html_en: At Unitree’s headquarters, the GD01 was displayed hanging from a reinforced steel joist. Wang was unwilling to operate it or even climb inside. “It might not be safe,” he shrugs of his creation, which carries a retail price of $650,000. When TIME’s photographer asked for a nearby promotional background to be relocated, Unitree’s technicians protested that shifting it would interfere with the lidar of the nearby phalanx of G1s practicing that now iconic Spring Festival Gala acrobatic martial-arts display. When the performance finished, those technicians rushed to spray their now sizzling lidar with coolant following that four-minute exertion.

sentences.en: At Unitree’s headquarters, the GD01 was displayed hanging from a reinforced steel joist.
sentences.zh: 在 Unitree 总部，GD01 悬挂在钢筋托梁上进行展示。

sentences.en: Wang was unwilling to operate it or even climb inside.
sentences.zh: 王某不愿意操作，甚至不愿意爬进去。

sentences.en: “It might not be safe,” he shrugs of his creation, which carries a retail price of $650,000.
sentences.zh: “这可能不安全，”他对自己的创作耸耸肩，该作品的零售价为 65 万美元。

sentences.en: When TIME’s photographer asked for a nearby promotional background to be relocated, Unitree’s technicians protested that shifting it would interfere with the lidar of the nearby phalanx of G1s practicing that now iconic Spring Festival Gala acrobatic martial-arts display.
sentences.zh: 当《时代》杂志的摄影师要求重新安置附近的宣传背景时，Unitree 的技术人员抗议称，移动背景会干扰附近 G1 方阵的激光雷达，该方阵正在练习现在标志性的春晚杂技武术表演。

sentences.en: When the performance finished, those technicians rushed to spray their now sizzling lidar with coolant following that four-minute exertion.
sentences.zh: 表演结束后，经过四分钟的努力，这些技术人员赶紧向他们现在炙手可热的激光雷达喷洒冷却剂。

blocks.html_en: For all Unitree’s lead in the humanoid market, today’s machines remain strangely theatrical: bodies dressed as intelligence, rehearsing a role they have not yet learned to live. “Humanoid robot technology is still in its relatively early stages,” cautions Wang. “It will likely take some time before we see genuine, large-scale deployment. It won’t happen overnight.” Nearby, GD01—part prototype, part prophecy—stands sentinel, as if quietly rebuking his master’s prudence.

sentences.en: For all Unitree’s lead in the humanoid market, today’s machines remain strangely theatrical: bodies dressed as intelligence, rehearsing a role they have not yet learned to live.
sentences.zh: 尽管 Unitree 在类人机器人市场上处于领先地位，但今天的机器仍然具有奇怪的戏剧性：身体装扮成智能，排练着他们尚未学会如何生活的角色。

sentences.en: “Humanoid robot technology is still in its relatively early stages,” cautions Wang.
sentences.zh: “人形机器人技术仍处于相对早期的阶段，”王警告说。

sentences.en: “It will likely take some time before we see genuine, large-scale deployment.
sentences.zh: “我们可能需要一段时间才能看到真正的大规模部署。

sentences.en: It won’t happen overnight.”
sentences.zh: 这不会在一夜之间发生。”

sentences.en: Nearby, GD01—part prototype, part prophecy—stands sentinel, as if quietly rebuking his master’s prudence.
sentences.zh: 附近，GD01——一半是原型，一半是预言——站在哨兵身边，仿佛在悄悄地斥责他主人的谨慎。

content_html_zh:
观众通常在结束 UFC 比赛时耳朵嗡嗡作响，并羞涩地决定返回体育馆。 王星星直奔自己的实验室。

“我认为我可以为拳击比赛制造一个更大的机器人，”杭州人形机器人公司 Unitree 的创始人兼首席执行官王说。 “然后我想，为什么不做一个更大的，实际上可以载人的呢？”

王的创意热情的成果是 GD01，Unitree 称其为世界上第一个批量生产的可变形机甲机器人。 站立 9 英尺。 它高、重达半吨，可以在安装在躯干上的驾驶舱内运送一名飞行员，可以用两条腿或四条腿行走，并挥动足以击倒墙壁的拳头。 重型机械铰链将钛合金肢体连接到碳纤维保护壳，使粗犷的红色腿能够平稳、从容地迈出步伐。

作为他首次国际媒体采访的一部分，王向《时代》杂志独家展示了 GD01，并透露除了综合格斗之外，他还从詹姆斯·卡梅隆那里获得了一些灵感：“我们实际上从《阿凡达》中的类似机器人中汲取了一些灵感。”

可以说，王在让这部科幻小说更接近现实方面所做的工作比任何人都多。 他是中国沿海城市宁波人，2016年年仅26岁就创立了Unitree，并从机器狗转向人形机器人，现在又推出了GD01人类移动机器。 尽管 GD01 可能会令人敬畏，但像 Unitree 的旗舰产品 G1 这样不起眼的人形机器人将成为全球机器人技术转型的主力，这场转型已经在中国、美国和其他地区加快步伐。

这是下一个伟大的技术前沿，之前一直属于《星球大战》和《银翼杀手》的范畴：能够行走、看、听并与现实世界进行物理交互的人工智能。 摩根士丹利预测，到 2035 年，我们身边将有多达 1300 万类人机器人，相当于玻利维亚的人口数量。 预计到 2050 年，这一数字将增至 10 亿，届时该行业的价值可能达到 5 万亿美元。

Unitree 并不是唯一一家在这一领域占据一席之地的公司。 今年 1 月，特斯拉发布了第三代 Optimus 人形机器人，目标是最终在弗里蒙特和德克萨斯州的工厂每年生产 1000 万台。 6 月下旬，总部位于加利福尼亚州的Figure 将其最新的 03 人形机器人运送到宝马位于南卡罗来纳州的斯帕坦堡工厂，其早期的 02 模型去年帮助组装了 30,000 辆汽车。 与此同时，总部位于俄勒冈州的 Agility 已在从丰田到拉丁美洲电子商务巨头 Mercado Libre 等各地进行部署。 但就数量而言，Unitree 处于全球领先地位，到 2025 年发货量将超过 5,500 台，占全球新兴市场的四分之一以上。 6 月，Nvidia 宣布其尖端的 Jetson Thor 芯片将安装到 Unitree 的 H2 人形机器人中，出售给全球研究机构。

Wang 说，我们可能距离类人机器人的“ChatGPT 时刻”只有 2 到 10 年的时间，届时机器人将实现足够的“泛化”，能够在 80% 的陌生环境中执行 80% 的请求语音命令。 “一旦技术超越这个...... 到了拐点，机器人就可以逐步推广到大规模商业用途。”他说。

如果说这一突破的时间表存在争议，那么它的潜在影响则不然。 不联合机器人（唯一的一种）可以在装配线上全天候运行，取代医院的部分护士，并在烈日下采摘水果。

积极的前景是，大规模人形机器人的采用将预示着一个前所未有的全球丰富时代，使平凡和危险的劳动实现自动化，同时迎来后稀缺社会。 然而，还有一种不那么乐观的占卜。 在大规模劳动力流失的情况下，机器人将更多的财富集中到更少的人手中。 随着生产与工资的分离，随着机器人开始主宰体力劳动和服务行业，人们可能会面临身份危机，而政府则面临着紧张的社会安全网以及美国和中国（人工智能领域的领先者）在原材料和技术方面日益激烈的竞争。 6月3日，三名美国议员提出了两党联合警卫法案，旨在禁止被视为对国家安全构成威胁的中国机器人。 该法案的共同发起人、众议院中国特别委员会主席约翰·穆勒纳尔(John Moolenaar)点名了Unitree，他指责王的公司从“慷慨的国家补贴”中受益，并威胁要让美国竞争对手破产。

借助人工智能，我们已经看到大型语言模型 (LLM) 解析大量数据的能力如何使它们容易产生幻觉和意外后果，例如设计生物武器。 但是，虽然法学硕士可能会带来伤害，但机器人却可以造成伤害，这预示着人工智能时代将出现可怕的新网络安全危险。 在线诈骗已经吸走了惊人的金额，但被黑客入侵的人形机器人可以将威胁从屏幕转移到家庭、街道、托儿所和工厂车间。

这些愿景之间的紧张关系可以归结为神话的选择：王和他的 GD01，以及他们的竞争对手，是类似于卢克·天行者和忠实、朴实的 C-3PO，还是更像弗兰肯斯坦和他的怪物？

不出所料，王转向积极的一面，他预测十年后每个家庭都可能拥有一个小型机器人，它可以洗衣服、烤牛排，甚至在你生病时提供基本护理。 他们还可以承担大多数人类宁愿避免的任务，从陡峭的山区耕种到下水道维护。

但如果这听起来像是一个未来到来的速度快于社会所能承受的速度，王坚称这种转变将是渐进的。 “它将逐步发展，”他说。 “如果社会发现机器人在某个领域表现不佳，或者人们根本不喜欢它们，我们可以轻松地将其应用转向其他领域。 因此，一切都在可控范围内。”

王不太可能是人工智能时代的预言家。 他身材瘦小，戴着眼镜，不像许多技术创始人那样表现得虚张声势。 相反，他以平静、坚定的信念谈论机器人技术。

自从他 10 岁时第一次观看波士顿动力公司创始人 Marc Raibert 在麻省理工学院进行的早期机器人实验以来，他就一直对人形革命的可能性和前景充满了兴趣。 他利用业余时间建造模型飞机、进行实验，不久之后还组装了微型涡轮喷气发动机。

认识 Unitree 的 GD01 机器人

2009年，王就读于杭州的浙江理工大学，主修机电工程。 他说，作为一名一年级学生，他用不到 30 美元建造了自己的第一个小型双足机器人，能够走几步。 同样的价格意识策略也支撑了 Unitree 的蓬勃发展。 在上海大学攻读硕士学位期间，他开发了一款四足机器人原型 XDog，至今仍在 Unitree 总部占据一席之地。 与他在波士顿动力公司的偶像所流行的液压动力设计不同，王选择了节省成本的电动机模型，这帮助XDog在全国创业大赛中获得了二等奖。

毕业并在深圳无人机先驱大疆创新工作一段时间后，王从一位天使投资人那里获得了 200 万元人民币（30 万美元）的资金，创立了 Unitree。 从那时起，它已成为一种文化现象；据估计，有 6 亿人观看了 Unitree 的 H2 和 G1 人形机器人在 2 月份的春节联欢晚会（相当于中国的超级碗中场秀）中挥舞双节棍和执行翻转的视频。 这是 Unitree 类人机器人第二年成为头条新闻，所有的目光都集中在王下一次的计划上。 “我不知道，”他笑着承认。

今年 3 月，Unitree 申请首次公开募股，该公司的估值将达到 60 亿美元，在中国大陆市场引发了上游供应商等受益公司的收购狂潮。

但过高的期望既是福也是祸。 虽然Unitree第一季度收入同比飙升超过68%，达到6200万美元，但同期调整后净利润却减少了一半，仅为600万美元，该公司将其归咎于研发和销售成本大幅上涨。 王谈到了网络病毒式传播的“另一面”。 “由于炒作如此激烈，这给我们公司和整个行业带来了相当大的压力，”他说。 “每个人都开始期待你的技术以惊人的速度飞跃发展。 但事实是，深度技术突破需要一定的时间。”

Unitree 的蓬勃发展正值中国政府开始向人工智能和机器人技术倾斜之际，习近平主席强调人工智能和机器人技术是“新增长动力”的关键例子。 中国工业和信息化部随后宣布，类人机器人是与智能手机和电动汽车同等水平的“颠覆性创新”，这些突破注定会改变人类的生产和生活方式，使领导力成为经济和国家安全的问题。 这是一项由现金支持的政策推动；据摩根士丹利称，自 2024 年底以来，中国城市已筹集了价值超过 260 亿美元的投资基金。 Unitree尚未透露其获得的国家支持的具体水平，但作为杭州“六小龙”（本地精英初创企业）之一，它受益于该市140亿美元的科技基金。 埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 在 1 月份的特斯拉财报电话会议上表示：“中国是一个令人兴奋的国家。” “据我们所知，我们在中国以外没有看到任何重要的[人形机器人]竞争对手。”去年春天，当王作为最年轻的成员出现在习近平主办的一场备受瞩目的商业研讨会上时，他在这一领域的领导作用就显而易见了。 现在有超过 140 家中国公司生产人形机器人，但中国内部的看法是 Unitree 不仅仅是另一家机器人初创公司，而且是一个硬件平台，注定要复制比亚迪在电动汽车方面取得的成就和大疆在无人机方面取得的成就。 王的策略很简单，但很难被击败：首先，控制最难的核心部件；利用规模不断降低成本；并利用每一代产品来开拓新市场。

Unitree 在短短 18 个月内将其旗舰产品 G1 人形机器人的税前价格从 16,000 美元削减至 13,500 美元。 （不过，根据 SemiAnalysis 最近的估计，由于组件成本低于 9,000 美元，Unitree 的毛利率高达 33%。） R1 型号目前的零售价低于 5,000 美元，六年来，他们的“机器狗”的价格已从 45,000 美元降至 2,000 美元以下。 Wang 坚持认为 Unitree 通过制造成本效率取胜，例如，它生产自己的执行器，即负责运动的“肌肉”，占人形机器人成本的 50% 至 70%。

虽然 G1 在承载和耐用性方面可能无法达到某些西方竞争对手的巅峰性能，但它已经跨越了实际部署的“足够好”门槛。 它能够连续承载 5 公斤有效负载 10 至 15 分钟而不会过热，因此正在走出研发实验室，进入仓库物流和物料搬运的试点部署。

也就是说，迄今为止，售出的人形机器人中只有一小部分用于实际有用的工作。 原因很简单：今天的机器人在执行一般工厂和仓库任务（例如堆垛和搬运箱子）时的工作效率约为人类的 30% 至 50%。 Unitree 的销售额中只有 9% 直接进入工业应用，17% 用于旅游景点、展览和零售店等商业场所。 这使得 Unitree 74% 的人形机器人销售给了大学、研究机构和希望磨练自己的人工智能和运动控制能力的个人开发者。 该公司并不孤单。 整个现代人形机器人行业本质上是在向试图制造更好机器人的人们出售机器人。 AI Proem 时事通讯的出版者 Grace Shao 表示：“人形机器人还没有做好进入大众市场的准备。”

然而，王并不担心，他将这一趋势与个人电脑的早期阶段进行了比较，当时个人电脑主要由企业人士购买：“随着软件变得越来越成熟，每年都有更多的开发人员加入，真正的工业部署比例正在以令人难以置信的速度增长。”

目前，Unitree 仍在与众多竞争对手一样面临同样的白鲸：泛化差距，王称这是“整个全球科学界最头疼的问题”。 尽管当今的后空翻机器堪称工程奇迹，但它们仍然顽固地针对特定任务。 如果你想让机器人拿起一支笔并在桌子上移动它，你必须对每一个细微的动作进行编程。 但为了让人工智能在现实世界中真正发挥作用，机器人必须从执行严格的脚本转变为理解新颖的命令——比如被告知“去杂货店”。

实现这一里程碑需要克服两个自我强化的问题：3D 数据的稀缺性和部署环境的多样性。 与 ChatGPT 等可以解析几乎无限的互联网的法学硕士不同，机器人需要在现实物理世界中收集的操作数据，这是昂贵且难以扩展的。 通常必须通过重复训练机器如何执行某些任务来手动收集它，例如折叠衬衫、给冰箱存货或摇晃麦泰酒。 另外，总部位于北京的人形机器人公司Noetix的首席执行官姜哲元解释说，“即使针对特定任务训练了性能良好的操纵策略，当环境发生轻微变化时，例如物体位移、不同的照明或不同的桌面材料，成功率往往会急剧下降。”

得益于像 Unitree 这样的公司，类人机器人的硬件正在快速发展。 真正的战场是机器人大脑或视觉语言动作（VLA）模型，这些模型旨在将人类输入转化为直观而准确地执行的具体人工智能。 市场情报公司 Kaiso Research 预测，到 2035 年，仅 VLA 领域的价值就将达到 405 亿美元，许多科技巨头都在积极开发自己的模型，包括 Nvidia 的 GR00T N1.7、Google DeepMind 的 RT-2-X 和阿里巴巴的 Qwen Robot Suite。

这场战斗越来越关注一件事：收集数据。 例如，在纽约市，Unitree 作为合作伙伴的 Micro AGI 正在派遣佩戴摄像头的工作人员免费清洁公寓，只是为了生成 3D 数据来训练其物理 AI 模型。 但作为一家专注的机器人公司，Unitree 的地位可以说比特斯拉、小米或小鹏汽车处于弱势，这些公司拥有多元化的收入来源并建立了供应链。 因此，解决这一普遍性差距是维持其当今市场冠军地位的关键。 毕竟，无论以何种标准衡量，其绝对销售额都微乎其微，这意味着无论谁先缩小差距，都可以轻松领先。

游戏规则的改变可能即将到来：革命性的新 6G 连接将使人工智能原生网络成为可能，集体学习可以在数百万台联网设备上无缝进行。 如果遥远仓库中的一个人形机器人努力研究一种新颖的工具并最终掌握它，那么新生成的数据可以立即上传并在整个全球舰队中共享。 市值 2000 亿美元的半导体公司高通首席执行官克里斯蒂亚诺·阿蒙 (Cristiano Amon) 告诉《时代》杂志，6G 将使我们能够“打造世界的数字孪生”。

增强的连接性将解决机器人的重量和电池寿命瓶颈。 探索我们的世界需要同时处理 4K 视频、空间激光雷达、音频和微妙的触觉或本体感受反馈。 但携带重型计算硬件及其供电方式严重限制了机器人的敏捷性和操作时间。 6G 可能会将云处理与近乎瞬时的 0.1 至 1 毫秒的网络延迟结合起来。 突然间，轻型物理机器人可以通过实时运行的基于云的人工智能大脑进行动画处理。 当机器人失手时，6G 通过远程人工遥控操作提供安全网。

随着这些机器上线，一个重大挑战将是保护任何集中式网络。 去年，网络安全分析师在 Unitree 的 Go1 机器狗中发现了一个“后门”，第三方可以通过该后门登录门户来查看机器人的位置、查看实时摄像头信息，甚至远程控制它。 作为回应，Unitree 关闭了导致漏洞的云服务。 王坚称他的“机器人产品完全脱离网络运行。 如果客户不允许，机器人将不会保存任何环境信息。” 此外，他补充说，与智能手机上保存的数据相比，机器人上存储的数据“完全可以忽略不计”。

还有一种恐惧，就像小说中常见的那样，为我们而制造的机器有一天可能会针对我们而行动。 即使是今天的机器人也已经表明，物理人工智能也存在物理风险：病毒剪辑捕捉到 Unitree 机器向毫无戒心的孩子挥动手臂或腿，导致他们误入编排的路径。 随着类人机器人逐渐走向通用化，它们是否会变得更加谨慎或更加危险是该行业面临的一个关键问题。 王淡化了叛逆机器人造成严重破坏的可能性。 “那不会发生，”他说。 “这些机器人的设计具有严格的硬件限制和安全保护。”

如果人形机器走下装配线并进入客厅，对安全的需求就会直击要害。 6 月 30 日，总部位于深圳的 UBtech 推出了专门用于陪伴的逼真 U1 人形机器人，具有硅胶皮肤和仿生面部表情，并由该公司声称的世界上第一个具有同理心的法学硕士提供支持，“能够识别 20 多种细粒度的情绪状态”。

但家庭人形机器人的最大障碍可能不是技术。 “恐怖谷”假说由日本机器人学家 Masahiro Mori 于 1970 年首次提出，它认为，当一个物体变得越来越像人类时，我们对它的喜爱就会增加。 一旦它跨过一个临界阈值——太接近人类——我们的反应就会陷入厌恶。 当各家公司尝试用真发拟人化机器人时，这个山谷能否弥合还有待观察。

在 Unitree 总部，GD01 悬挂在钢筋托梁上进行展示。 王某不愿意操作，甚至不愿意爬进去。 “这可能不安全，”他对自己的创作耸耸肩，该作品的零售价为 65 万美元。 当《时代》杂志的摄影师要求重新安置附近的宣传背景时，Unitree 的技术人员抗议称，移动背景会干扰附近 G1 方阵的激光雷达，该方阵正在练习现在标志性的春晚杂技武术表演。 表演结束后，经过四分钟的努力，这些技术人员赶紧向他们现在炙手可热的激光雷达喷洒冷却剂。

尽管 Unitree 在类人机器人市场上处于领先地位，但今天的机器仍然具有奇怪的戏剧性：身体装扮成智能，排练着他们尚未学会如何生活的角色。 “人形机器人技术仍处于相对早期的阶段，”王警告说。 “我们可能需要一段时间才能看到真正的大规模部署。 这不会在一夜之间发生。” 附近，GD01——一半是原型，一半是预言——站在哨兵身边，仿佛在悄悄地斥责他主人的谨慎。
